misconceptionSummary: Students identify a common factor inconsistently or factorise only part of the expression.

steps.narration: To factorise into one bracket, find a factor that both terms share.
The terms six x and nine both share a factor of three, so we can factor out three.
Divide each term by the common factor. Six x divided by three is two x, and nine divided by three is three.
Now write the common factor outside the bracket and the divided terms inside the bracket.

visuals.expression: Find a common factor, divide each term, then write one bracket
visuals.label: 6x + 9 = 3(2x + 3)

steps.narration: That is the factorising method. Factorising is the reverse of expanding.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: putting the common factor outside but forgetting to divide both terms inside the bracket. Every term must be divided by the factor taken out.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: If you factor out three, both terms must shrink by a factor of three inside the bracket.
